questions.questionText: On non-contrast CT head, acute blood appears:
questions.optionB: Hyperdense (bright white — 50-70 HU for acute blood due to globin protein component)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Acute blood is HYPERdense (white) on NCCT. Why correct: Fresh blood has high protein content (hemoglobin) → 50-70 HU = white. Subacute (1-3 weeks) → isodense. Chronic (>3 weeks) → hypodense (dark). Why A wrong: Hypodense = CSF, chronic collections. Why C wrong: Isodense = subacute blood (hardest to detect). Why D wrong: CT is primarily used for detecting acute hemorrhage. Clinical relevance: CT is first-line for suspected acute hemorrhage (fast, widely available). Exam trap: Subacute SDH can be isodense to brain — easily missed. Look for mass effect/midline shift. Memory aid: 'Acute = Bright (high density), Chronic = dark (low density).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – CT.

questions.questionText: Hounsfield unit values: air = -1000, fat = -100, water = 0, soft tissue = 20-80, bone = 400-1000. What is the approximate HU value of acute hemorrhage?
questions.explanation: Core concept: HU scale enables tissue characterization on CT. Why correct: Acute blood ≈ 50-70 HU (protein content)/ Why A wrong: 0 HU = water/CSF. Why C wrong: -100 HU = fat. Why D wrong: 400+ HU = bone/calcification. Clinical relevance: HU helps identify tissue — adrenal adenoma <10 HU, renal cyst <20 HU. Exam trap: Know the basic HU values — frequently tested as table-based questions. Memory aid: 'A-F-W-S-B scale: Air(-1000), Fat(-100), Water(0), Soft tissue(20-80), Bone(400+).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – CT.

questions.questionText: A patient with abdominal trauma. CT abdomen with contrast shows a shattered left kidney with active contrast extravasation and large perinephric hematoma. According to AAST, this is classified as:
questions.optionB: Grade IV-V (shattered kidney with active bleeding = high-grade injury requiring urgent intervention)
questions.explanation: Core concept: AAST renal injury grading guides management. Why correct: Grade IV: renal laceration extending into collecting system OR segmental vascular injury. Grade V: shattered kidney or renal vascular pedicle injury/avulsion. Active contrast extravasation = vascular injury. Why A wrong: Grade II = laceration <1cm, no extravasation. Why C wrong: Grade I = contusion or subcapsular hematoma. Why D wrong: Grade III = laceration >1cm without collecting system involvement. Clinical relevance: Grade I-III: conservative management. Grade IV-V: may need angioembolization or surgery. Exam trap: Active arterial contrast blush (extravasation) on CT = active bleeding = urgent. Memory aid: 'Shattered + Spillage = Severe (Grade V).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – CT.
questions.conceptTag: Renal injury AAST grading CT trauma

questions.questionText: HRCT of the chest in a patient with IPF (usual interstitial pneumonia) characteristically shows:
questions.optionB: Bilateral basal subpleural honeycombing with traction bronchiectasis and minimal ground-glass opacity
questions.explanation: Core concept: UIP/IPF on HRCT = basal, subpleural honeycombing + traction bronchiectasis. Why correct: IPF — progressive fibrotic ILD. HRCT pattern: bilateral, basilar, peripheral/subpleural distribution. Honeycombing (thick-walled cystic airspaces) = definite UIP → no biopsy needed. Traction bronchiectasis = fibrosis pulling airways. Why A wrong: Upper lobe GGO = sarcoidosis, HP (some forms). Why C wrong: Central GGO = pulmonary edema pattern. Why D wrong: Tree-in-bud = infectious bronchiolitis, TB. Clinical relevance: HRCT with definite UIP pattern avoids need for surgical lung biopsy. Nintedanib/pirfenidone slow progression. Exam trap: Honeycombing = irreversible fibrosis; Ground-glass = potentially reversible inflammation. Memory aid: 'IPF = Inferior (basal) Peripheral Fibrosis (honeycombing).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – CT.
questions.conceptTag: IPF UIP HRCT honeycombing basal subpleural

questions.questionText: CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) is the investigation of choice for:
questions.optionB: Pulmonary embolism (hypodense filling defect within contrast-enhanced pulmonary arteries)
questions.explanation: Core concept: CTPA = gold standard for diagnosing pulmonary embolism. Why correct: IV contrast → opacifies pulmonary arteries. PE appears as hypodense (dark) filling defect within bright artery. Sensitivity >95%, specificity >97%. Why A wrong: Bronchitis — clinical diagnosis, CXR findings minimal. Why C wrong: Pneumonia — CXR and routine CT sufficient. Why D wrong: Effusion — CXR/USG adequate. Clinical relevance: D-dimer if clinical probability low (Wells score). If D-dimer positive → CTPA. RV strain sign (RV:LV ratio >1) indicates massive PE. Exam trap: V/Q scan is alternative if contrast allergy or renal failure. Normal CTPA effectively rules out PE. Memory aid: 'CTPA = Clot Tracking in Pulmonary Arteries.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – CT.

questions.questionText: A 50-year-old man with a liver lesion on CT showing arterial enhancement with portal venous phase washout and delayed enhancing capsule. In a background of cirrhosis, this is diagnostic of:
questions.optionB: Hepatocellular carcinoma (arterial hyperenhancement + washout + capsule in cirrhotic liver = LIRADS-5, definite HCC)
questions.explanation: Core concept: In cirrhosis, arterial enhancement + washout + capsule = HCC (no biopsy needed). Why correct: HCC has arterial supply (unlike normal liver which is portal). LIRADS-5 criteria: >10mm + arterial hyperenhancement + one or more of: washout, enhancing capsule, threshold growth. Why A wrong: Hemangioma — centripetal fill-in (not washout). Why C wrong: FNH — central scar enhances on delayed (not washout). Why D wrong: Metastases — typically ring enhancement, not this pattern. Clinical relevance: LIRADS system eliminates biopsy for clear imaging criteria. AFP + imaging for surveillance in cirrhosis. Exam trap: Washout = HCC; Fill-in = hemangioma. Direction of enhancement change is key. Memory aid: 'HCC = Hyper Arterial then Cancels out (washout).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – CT.
questions.conceptTag: HCC arterial washout capsule LIRADS cirrhosis

questions.questionText: Stanford Type A aortic dissection on CT angiography is defined as:
questions.optionB: Dissection involving the ascending aorta (regardless of extension) — surgical emergency
questions.explanation: Core concept: Stanford A = ascending aorta involved = SURGERY. B = only descending = MEDICAL. Why correct: Type A involves ascending aorta (DeBakey I: ascending → descending; DeBakey II: ascending only). Surgical emergency (mortality 1-2%/hour untreated). CTA: intimal flap dividing true and false lumen. Why A wrong: Descending only = Type B (medical management unless complicated). Why C wrong: Arch involvement alone is rare; if ascending involved → Type A. Why D wrong: Only abdominal = Type B variant. Clinical relevance: CTA sensitivity >95% for aortic dissection. Exam trap: Stanford classification — A = Ascending (Always surgery); B = Below the subclavian (Blood pressure control). Memory aid: 'A = Ascending = Always operate.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – CT.
questions.conceptTag: Aortic dissection Stanford A ascending CTA
